title: What Is Kefir?
subtitle: A living symbiotic culture that puts yogurt's probiotic count to shame

image.caption: A glass of freshly strained milk kefir — tangy, creamy, and teeming with life

facts.label: Type
facts.value: SCOBY-based ferment (symbiotic culture of bacteria and yeast)

facts.label: Base liquids
facts.value: Milk (cow, goat, buffalo) or sugar water

sections.content: Kefir is a tangy, slightly effervescent fermented drink produced by adding kefir grains — a unique symbiotic culture of bacteria and yeast (SCOBY) — to milk or sugar water [cite:c1]. Unlike yogurt, which typically contains 2–7 bacterial strains, kefir harbours a complex ecosystem of over 50 species of lactic acid bacteria, acetic acid bacteria, and yeasts working in concert. The result is a drink with unparalleled probiotic diversity, a pleasantly sour flavour, and a creamy or fizzy texture depending on whether you're making [[article:milk-kefir-vs-water-kefir]]. Originating in the Caucasus Mountains thousands of years ago, kefir has spread across the globe and is now one of the most popular home ferments worldwide.

The word "kefir" actually refers to two quite different fermented drinks made with two biologically distinct sets of grains. Milk kefir grains ferment animal milk (or sometimes coconut milk) into a thick, tangy, yogurt-like beverage. Water kefir grains (also called tibicos) ferment sugar water, fruit juice, or coconut water into a light, fizzy probiotic soda. Despite sharing the name, the two grain types are composed of different microbial communities and are not interchangeable [[article:milk-kefir-vs-water-kefir]].

sections.caption: Quick comparison: milk kefir vs water kefir
rows: Contains dairy (some lactose-intolerant people tolerate it)

sections.content: Kefir grains are not cereal grains — they are small, gelatinous masses composed of a polysaccharide matrix called kefiran, within which dozens of species of bacteria and yeast live in a stable, self-perpetuating symbiosis [cite:c2]. This makes kefir grains a true SCOBY (Symbiotic Culture of Bacteria and Yeast), similar in concept to a kombucha mother but structurally and microbiologically distinct. The kefiran matrix provides a physical scaffold that holds the microbial community together and protects it from environmental stress. When placed in fresh milk or sugar water, the grains metabolise sugars, produce lactic acid and CO₂, and gradually grow — doubling in mass roughly every 1–2 weeks under ideal conditions.

sections.title: Kefiran — More Than Just Structure
sections.content: Kefiran, the polysaccharide that forms the grain matrix, is itself a bioactive compound. Research suggests it has antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and wound-healing properties [cite:c3]. When you consume kefir, you're ingesting dissolved kefiran along with the live bacteria — a benefit that probiotic capsules simply cannot replicate.

Milk kefir is nutritionally similar to the milk it's made from, but fermentation enhances several aspects. LAB partially digest lactose (making kefir tolerable for many lactose-intolerant individuals), synthesise B vitamins and vitamin K2, and increase the bioavailability of calcium and other minerals [cite:c4]. The following values are approximate for one cup (240 ml) of whole-milk kefir.

sections.caption: Approximate nutrition per 240 ml whole-milk kefir

sections.title: Ready to Make Your First Batch?
sections.content: Making kefir at home is one of the simplest fermentation projects you can start. All you need is a jar, some kefir grains (ask a friend, join a local BLR fermentation group, or order online), and milk or sugar water. Check out [[recipe:milk-kefir-basic]] for a step-by-step dairy kefir recipe, or [[recipe:water-kefir-basic]] if you prefer a dairy-free fizzy drink. For grain maintenance tips, see [[article:kefir-grain-care]].
